key_insight: Thermal spray coatings can be engineered from the process level up to create micro/nanostructured electrochemical interfaces that reduce interfacial resistance and improve durability across batteries, fuel cells, and electrolyzers.
engineering_relevance: The review provides cross-system design rules for using thermal spray process control to tune porosity, phase distribution, and bonding for longer-lasting, more scalable energy-device coatings.
method: Compared major thermal spray routes: atmospheric plasma spray, suspension plasma spray, high-velocity oxy-fuel, and cold spray.
Mapped process parameters to coating microstructure and interfacial chemistry using a process-structure-property framework.
Highlighted advanced strategies such as functionally graded interfaces, adaptive coating systems, and machine learning-guided development.
evidence_signal: The paper synthesizes prior studies across batteries, PEM and alkaline fuel cells, and SOFC/SOEC systems to evaluate how process-induced microstructure affects transport, resistance, and stability.
watch_item: Performance gains may be limited by porosity, weak inter-splat bonding, and non-equilibrium phase changes that are difficult to control consistently at scale.
paper_title: Thermal spray coatings in batteries, electrolyzers and solid oxide electrochemical systems
